section.heading: Term of License and License Renewal.

para.text: Licenses converted under Alcoholic Beverages and Cannabis Article, §36-403, Annotated Code of Maryland, are valid for 5 years from the date of the initial conversion fee payment to the Administration.

As a condition of licensure, a licensee shall comply with all subregulatory guidance issued by the Administration, including but not limited to bulletins, notices, resolutions, seed-to-sale tracking system guidance, and technical authorities posted to the Administration’s website.
A cannabis license under this chapter is valid for:
5 years on initial licensure; and
5 years on renewal.
At least 90 calendar days before the expiration of a license, the Administration shall notify the licensee of the:
Date on which the license expires;
Process and the fee required to renew the license; and
Consequences of a failure to renew the license.
At least 30 calendar days before a license expires, a licensee seeking approval shall submit:
The renewal application in the form designated by the Administration;

Proof that fingerprints have been submitted to Criminal Justice Information System and the Federal Bureau of Investigation for each agent and any owner with an ownership interest of 5 percent or more;
To a full inspection of the licensed premises as described in COMAR 14.17.14.03, unless a full inspection was satisfactorily completed within 3 months before the date of the license expiration; and
Payment of the fee specified in COMAR 14.17.21.
The Administration may renew a license that meets the requirements for renewal as stated in §E of this regulation.
If a licensee fails the inspection or submits a deficient application for renewal, the licensee may apply for reinstatement by:
Submitting a plan to correct the deficiencies noted during an inspection; and
Amending the application for renewal.
The Administration may deny a license renewal if:
The plan to correct deficiencies identified in an inspection is deficient;
The amended application for renewal is deficient;
The licensee is repeatedly found in violation of health and safety regulations during the license period; or
The licensee is repeatedly found in violation of this subtitle or Title 36, Alcoholic Beverages and Cannabis Article, Annotated Code of Maryland.
A licensee who fails to apply for license renewal by the date specified by the Administration, or whose license was not renewed by the Administration:
Shall cease operations at all premises; and
May not provide cannabis to any entity or individual.
A license may be reinstated upon:
Payment of the reinstatement fee specified in COMAR 14.17.21; and
Submission of a reinstatement application approved by the Administration.
A licensee may not register with the State Department of Assessment and Taxation using a legal name that:
Uses the terms “cannabis”, “marijuana”, or other synonym related to controlled substances;
Suggests the use of cannabis as an intoxicant; or
Incorporates any copyrighted material or trademark or service mark attributable to another entity.
The Administration may specify the format and manner for all required licensee and conditional licensee submissions.

section.heading: Transfer of Ownership Interest in a License.

para.text: A cannabis licensee, including a cannabis licensee whose license was converted in accordance with Alcoholic Beverages and Cannabis Article, §36-401, Annotated Code of Maryland, is not transferable except as provided under Alcoholic Beverages and Cannabis Article, §36-503, Annotated Code of Maryland.

If a proposed transfer is for less than 5 percent of a cannabis licensee and the proposed transfer will not result in the transferee holding 5 percent or more of the licensee, then the proposed transfer does not require approval by the Administration.
The Administration may approve a transfer or assignment of ownership if:

The Administration receives notice of the intent of the owner of the interest, or of the estate of the owner of the interest, to transfer or assign an ownership interest in a license to another party, in a form prescribed by the Administration;
The proposed transfer does not violate Alcoholic Beverages and Cannabis Article, Title 36, Annotated Code of Maryland;
The transferee has paid the required fee specified in COMAR 14.17.21; and
The transferee has provided criminal history record and financial information to the Administration.
The Administration shall deny transfer of an interest for any proposed transferee:
If the transfer violates COMAR 14.17.16;

If the transfer would convey a controlling interest in the license prior to the cannabis licensee being licensed and operational for a period of at least 5 years, and is not proposed due to death, disability, incapacity, bankruptcy, or receivership in accordance with a lending agreement of a cannabis licensee or court order; or

If, prior to the cannabis licensee being licensed and operational for a period of at least 5 years, the transfer would convey to the transferee the right or authority to obtain a controlling interest in the license at a later date.
The Administration may deny transfer of an interest for any proposed transferee if the:

Transferee or an individual with ownership or control of the transferee has been convicted of or pleaded nolo contendere to a crime involving moral turpitude, whether or not any appeal or other proceeding is pending to have the conviction or plea set aside;
Payment of taxes due in any jurisdiction is in arrears.
The Administration may deny transfer of a controlling interest in a license or in an ownership interest of 5 percent or more if the Administration finds good cause to deny the proposed transfer.

Any individual or entity identified as having ownership or control of a license may not hold an ownership interest that exceeds the limitations set forth in Alcoholic Beverages and Cannabis Article, §36-401(e), Annotated Code of Maryland.

If the Administration approves a transfer of an ownership interest of a license and subsequently the Administration finds that such a transfer violates State, local, or federal law, the Administration may:
Issue a fine against any parties involved in the transfer;
Declare the transfer void; and
Rescind the license.
Prior to the transfer, the licensee shall conduct a full inventory of all cannabis and cannabis products, in a manner prescribed by the Administration.
Employee Incentive Programs.

In this section, the term “incentive unit” means a unit of ownership in the licensee or its parent, subsidiary, or affiliate such as stock or limited liability company membership, issued to an individual as part of a licensee’s employee incentive program or other compensation.
A licensee may issue employee incentive units as part of an employee compensation plan.
A licensee shall:
Establish a detailed employee incentive plan for each individual to whom it intends to issue incentive units and issue incentive units in accordance with the plan; and
Upon request, provide a copy of the plan in §J(3)(a) of this regulation, in a manner prescribed by the Administration.
A licensee may not issue employee incentive units in a way that violates:
This regulation;
This chapter;
This subtitle;
Alcoholic Beverages and Cannabis Article, Title 36, Annotated Code of Maryland.
Incentive units issued to employees under this section may not:
Exceed 5 percent total ownership to any one individual; or
Change the control structure of the license.

In the annual disclosure of ownership and control report required by COMAR 14.17.16.02, a licensee shall disclose any employee incentive unit issuances or transfers that have occurred since the date of the licensee's last annual disclosure submission.
Definitions.
In this regulation, the following terms have the meaning indicated.
Terms Defined. “Management company” means an entity that provides management services to a licensed entity.
Management Agreement.

“Management agreement” means an arrangement between a management company and a licensee for the provision of services, including but not limited to consulting, advisory, or marketing services related to the licensee’s operations.
“Management agreement” does not include arrangements for legal, financial, or other services that do not delegate operational aspects of the business to the service provider.
“Management company” means an entity that provides management services to a licensed entity.
A management company shall be:
Approved by the Administration, in a manner prescribed by the Administration; and
In good standing with the State Department of Assessments and Taxation.
A licensee shall provide a copy of a management agreement to the Administration and include:
Information detailing any compensation paid in exchange for the management services;
Criminal history record and financial information of the third party providing the management services; and
Any other information relevant to the management agreement requested by the Administration.
A management agreement may not take effect unless the Administration has:
Received proper notice of the management agreement, as specified in §C of this regulation;
Received the required fee specified in COMAR 14.17.21; and
Approved the management agreement.
The Administration may deny a management agreement:
If the management agreement constitutes an invalid transfer of an ownership interest in a license, as specified in Regulation .04 of this chapter;

If the criminal history record information or the background investigation reveals the personnel of a third party providing the management services has been convicted of or pleaded nolo contendere to a crime involving moral turpitude, whether or not any appeal or other proceeding is pending to have the conviction or plea set aside;
If the management agreement transfers control of a license:
In violation of Alcoholic Beverages and Cannabis Article, §36-503, Annotated Code of Maryland; or
As established in §H of this regulation;
If the licensee fails to produce additional documentation requested by the Administration;
If the management agreement is found to be in violation of §G of this regulation; or
For any other good cause.
In the case of material change to a management agreement, including but not limited to a change of ownership or control of the management company, the licensee shall:
Provide any relevant records, files, or information to the Administration; and
Receive Administration approval prior to the material change to the management agreement taking effect.
A management agreement under this regulation may not require a licensee to:

Operate the business under a marketing plan or system that is associated with the trademark, service mark, trade name, logotype, advertising, or other commercial symbol that designates the licensee as same or similar to the management company;
Offer, sell, or distribute cannabis or cannabis products under a marketing plan or system prescribed in substantial part by the entity providing the management agreement;
Limit the offerings of cannabis or cannabis products to another licensed entity holding a direct or indirect financial relationship to the management company; or
Sell or transfer ownership interest in the license at a later date to the management company.
A management agreement may constitute a transfer of control if it conveys to a management company:
Unilateral control as defined in COMAR 14.17.01; or
The right or authority to make major marketing, production, and financial decisions, including:
The right or authority to operate the business under a trademark, service mark, trade name, logotype, or other commercial symbol that is the same as the management company or another licensee;
The right or authority to purchase a controlling interest in, or control of, the license at a later date; or
The right to or actual payment from the licensee over the course of a calendar year exceeding the greatest of:
25 percent of the licensee’s gross revenue;
50 percent of the licensee’s net profits; or
In this regulation, the following terms have the meanings indicated.
Terms Defined.
“Co-located licensee” means a grower or processor licensee who has entered an agreement to share a licensed premises.
“Commingling” means mixing any cannabis belonging to one licensee with cannabis that belongs to another licensee.
“Storage area” means any single room or container designated for cannabis to be kept before distribution, transfer, or processing.
A maximum of three licensees may enter an agreement to share a licensed premises, provided:
Provided at least one of the licensees operates in the region in which they were awarded a license; and
The licensees are growers or processors.
If co-located licensees share equipment or production materials, each licensee shall:
Use equipment at separate times;
Sanitize any equipment and work space before it is used by another licensee; and
Maintain independent and unique storage areas.
A co-located licensee shall:
Adhere to all requirements for operating a licensed grower or processor premises, whichever applies;
Establish and follow all standard operating procedures for sharing a licensed premises and, if applicable, sharing equipment;
Store and keep separate all of its cannabis at all times to avoid commingling at any stage of the growth or production process;
Have equal, independent, and unique control over access to its storage area, including the ability to add or remove its badged agents to access any area in which their plants or products are stored;
Have equal independent and unique access to its video surveillance recordings, which shall be recorded and saved as required by COMAR 14.17.10.02 or COMAR 14.17.11.02, whichever applies; and
Maintain independent and accurate records within the seed-to-sale tracking system as required by regulations for their license type.
A co-location agreement:
May not transfer ownership or control of a license in violation of Alcoholic Beverages and Cannabis Article, §36-503, Annotated Code of Maryland;
May not be used to circumvent any requirements for operating a licensed premises;
May not take effect prior to Administration:
Approval of the agreement terms; and
Inspection of the shared premises; and

If co-located licensees are sharing security or video surveillance systems, shall specify which licensee is responsible for ensuring a shared system complies with COMAR 14.17.10.02F and G or COMAR 14.17.11.02E and F, whichever apply.
Upon signing a co-location agreement, a co-locating licensee shall provide to the Administration:
A copy of the co-location agreement; and
Any other documents relevant to the co-location as requested by the Administration.
If the Administration determines a co-location agreement violates §F of this regulation, the Administration may:
Deny or rescind approval of the co-location agreement; and
Fine, suspend, restrict, revoke, or otherwise sanction a co-locating licensee.

para.text: When a visitor is admitted to an area of the premises used for cannabis operations, an agent shall:
Log the visitor in and out;
Retain with the log a photocopy of the visitor’s government-issued identification;
Continuously, physically supervise the visitor while they are on the premises; and
Ensure that the visitor does not touch any cannabis.
An Administration investigator is not subject to the visitor requirements established in §A of this regulation.
The grower shall maintain a log of all visitors for 2 years.

A firefighter, a rescue squad member, law enforcement officer, or emergency medical services personnel who is actively responding to a call for emergency services is not subject to §A(1)—(3) of this regulation.
A grower shall establish written standard operating procedures in accordance with this subtitle to include all aspects of:
Inventory control, including:
Identifying and tracking the grower’s stock of cannabis in the seed-to-sale tracking system; and
Monthly physical inventory;
Receiving material used to produce cannabis;
Shipping and transporting cannabis or products containing cannabis;
Sanitation, including:
Storage of cannabis;
Maintaining the sanitation of equipment that comes into contact with cannabis; and
Good hygiene practices and the wearing of protective clothing by agents;
Equipment maintenance and calibration;
Handling, packaging, and labeling of cannabis products and byproducts;
Rework or reprocessing;
Green waste procedures;
Non-green waste procedures;
Security and visitor procedures, including theft and diversion prevention;
Storage, handling, and disposal of recalled materials.
Irrigation, propagation, cultivation, fertilization, including the use of growing media or hydroponic solution;
Recording the cultivation process;
Pest monitoring and management;
Harvesting, drying, curing;
Research and development procedures;
Decontamination, including but not limited to:
Preventing contamination;
Investigating decontaminating after repeated tests falling outside action limits; and
Remediation steps and resulting environmental analysis.
If applicable, co-locating with another licensee, including:
Sharing equipment and production materials; and
Maintaining independent and unique security video surveillance control.
A grower’s standard operating procedures shall:
Be available to each agent in a form the agent understands;
Be available on-site for inspection by the Administration; and
Accurately reflect the procedures used at the premises.

para.text: A processor shall:

Create and enter timely and accurate data into the seed-to-sale tracking system to identify and track the processor’s stock of cannabis from the time it is delivered or produced to the time it is delivered to another licensee; and
At least monthly, conduct a physical inventory of the stock and compare the physical inventory of stock with the stock reflected in the seed-to-sale tracking system.
Receipt of Products Containing Cannabis.
A processor may not:
Acquire cannabis from an individual or entity in Maryland other than a licensee;
Acquire cannabis from outside of Maryland unless authorized by the Administration; or
Transport cannabis to any place outside of Maryland.
Hemp.
A processor may acquire hemp, as defined in Agriculture Article, §14-101, Annotated Code of Maryland, from a person licensed to produce hemp by:
The Maryland Department of Agriculture;
The Secretary of the U.S. Department of Agriculture; or
An agency of another state pursuant to a hemp production plan that has been approved by the Secretary of the U.S. Department of Agriculture.
Any product derived from hemp shall comply with:
The testing standards established in COMAR 14.17.08 and the Technical Authority;
The relevant manufacturing standards established in this chapter and COMAR 14.17.13; and
The relevant packaging and labeling standards established in COMAR 14.17.18.
A licensed processor shall:
After placing an order to acquire hemp and before accepting delivery of hemp, submit a request for Administration approval to acquire hemp in the manner specified by the Administration.
Follow the process for receiving cannabis established in this regulation.
Sanitary Storage of Cannabis. A processor shall:
Maintain the cleanliness of any building or equipment used to store or display cannabis;
Ensure cannabis is free from contamination;
Require a processor agent to report any personal health condition that might compromise the cleanliness or quality of the cannabis the processor agent might handle;
Dispose and segregate storage of any cannabis:
That is outdated, damaged, deteriorated, misbranded, or adulterated; or
Whose containers or packages have been improperly or accidentally opened.
Equipment Sanitation, Accuracy, and Maintenance of Logs. A processor shall:
Maintain the sanitation of equipment that comes in contact with cannabis;
Ensure all scales are National Type Evaluation Program-approved;
Ensure that any scale, balance, or other measurement device and any automatic, mechanical, or electronic equipment is:

Annually calibrated by a calibration laboratory accredited to International Organization for Standardization (ISO) standard 17025 ISO/IEC by an accreditation body that is signatory to the International Laboratory Accreditation Cooperation (ILAC) Mutual Recognition Arrangement; and
Checked at least once each month to ensure accuracy;
Maintain an accurate log recording the:
Cleaning of equipment;
The maintenance of equipment; and
The calibration of equipment; and
Make records and certifications required under §D(2) and (3) of this regulation available to the Administration available upon request.
Sanitation.
Scales, surfaces, and other equipment use for the purposes of creating usable cannabis products shall be cleaned and sanitized:
Between shifts;
Between packaging, repackaging, or otherwise creation of usable cannabis products with different usable cannabis batches;
Before beginning any packaging, repackaging, or otherwise creation of usable cannabis products; and
After the completion of any packaging or creation of usable cannabis products.
A processor shall maintain an accurate log of cleaning and sanitation required under §E(1) of this regulation.
Controls for Processing of Cannabis Concentrates and Cannabis-Infused Products.
A licensed processor of cannabis concentrates and cannabis-infused products shall:
Develop standard operating procedures in accordance with COMAR 14.17.11.19, good manufacturing practices, and a training plan before producing cannabis concentrates and cannabis-infused products;
Require that any person involved in processing cannabis concentrates and cannabis-infused products:
Is appropriately trained in accordance with their job description to safely operate and maintain the system used for processing;
Has direct access to applicable material safety sheets and labels; and
Follows OSHA protocols for handling and storage of all chemicals;
Assign a unique lot number to each lot of cannabis concentrate or cannabis-infused product;
If using a solvent-based extraction method, ensure solvents are at least 99 percent pure;
Require:
The use of solvents in a professional grade, closed-loop extraction system designed to recover the solvents;
Work in a spark-free environment with proper ventilation; and
Following all applicable OSHA regulations, and local fire, safety, and building codes in the processing and storage of the solvents; and
If using carbon dioxide gas extraction, ensure:
Every vessel is rated to a minimum of 900 pounds per square inch;
The use of a professional grade, closed-loop system; and
The use of carbon dioxide that is at least 99 percent pure.
A licensed processor may use heat, screens, presses, steam distillation, ice water, and other methods to produce cannabis concentrates.

Upon successful completion of a validation process, the licensed processor shall use an Administration-registered independent testing laboratory to test each unique lot of cannabis concentrate or cannabis-infused product and obtain a certificate of analysis.
Batch Release Controls.
If a licensed processor, upon review of the certificate of analysis, determines that a lot meets the specification for the product, the licensed processor may:
Assign an expiration date to the lot;
Release the lot for distribution; and
Revise the status of the lot in the seed-to-sale tracking system.
If a licensed processor receives test results that the lot falls outside action limits, the licensed processor:
May rework or reprocess the lot according to their standard operating procedure; and
If applicable, shall ensure the reworked or reprocessed lot is resampled and retested by the independent testing laboratory to meet all required specifications.
A licensed processor shall retain every certificate of analysis.
All cannabis products shall have a certificate of analysis, as specified in COMAR 14.17.08.05A(5), prior to transfer to a licensed dispensary.
Stability Testing and Retention Sampling.
A processor shall provide a sample from each unique lot to an independent testing laboratory sufficient to perform stability testing at 6-month intervals to:
Ensure product potency and purity; and
Provide support for expiration dating.
Retention samples retained from each released lot shall be:
Tested by the State Cannabis Testing Laboratory following an adverse event reported to the Administration;
Properly stored by the licensed processor; and
Properly discarded 6 months after the expiration date of the lot.
Correction and Prevention Action.
If a processor’s test results indicate the confirmed presence of a pathogen more than once in a 60-day period, the processor shall:
Conduct environmental swab testing as required by the Administration’s Technical Authority;
Conduct an investigation to determine the root cause of contamination;
Decontaminate all areas, including rooms, equipment, and surfaces used for cannabis product processing;
Provide, in the manner designated by the Administration:
A report of the investigation; and
The licensee’s corrective action plan; and
If applicable, update and implement standard operating procedures needed to ensure passing samples in the future.
The Administration may require a processor to:
Conduct further investigation; and
Implement additional procedures to prevent future contamination.
When a visitor is admitted to an area of the premises used for cannabis operations, a registered agent shall:
Log the visitor in and out;
Retain with the log a photocopy of the visitor’s government-issued identification;
Continuously, physically supervise the visitor while they are on the premises; and
Ensure that the visitor does not touch any cannabis.
An Administration investigator is not subject to the visitor requirements established in §A of this regulation.
The processor shall maintain a log of all visitors for 2 years.

A firefighter, a rescue squad member, law enforcement officer, or emergency medical services personnel who is actively responding to a call for emergency services is not subject to §A(1)—(3) of this regulation.
A processor shall establish standard operating procedures for all aspects of:
Inventory control, including:
Identifying and tracking the processor’s stock of cannabis in the seed-to-sale tracking system; and
Monthly physical inventory;
Receiving, shipping, and transporting cannabis or products containing cannabis;
Sanitation, including:
Storage of cannabis;
Maintaining the sanitation of equipment that comes into contact with cannabis; and
Good hygiene practices and the wearing of protective clothing by agents;
Equipment maintenance and calibration;
Handling, packaging, and labeling cannabis and cannabis products;
Reworking or reprocessing a lot if the processor receives a test result that the lot does not meet specifications;
Green waste procedures;
Security and visitor procedures, including theft and diversion procedures;
Storage, handling, and disposing of recalled materials;
Producing cannabis concentrates and cannabis-infused products, including:
Methods, equipment, solvents, and gases when processing cannabis concentrates and cannabis-infused products;
Use of solvents in a professional grade, closed-loop extraction system designed to recover the solvents;
Work in a spark-free environment with proper ventilation;
Following all applicable OSHA regulations, and local fire, safety, and building codes in the processing and storage of the solvents; and
If applicable, carbon dioxide extraction;
Decontamination, including but not limited to:
Preventing contamination;
Investigating decontaminating after presumptive positive pathogen detection; and
Remediation steps and resulting environmental analysis.
If applicable, producing edible cannabis products, including receipt of ingredients, storage, equipment maintenance, sanitation, manufacture, and transport; and
If applicable, co-locating with another licensee, including:
Sharing equipment and production materials; and
Maintaining independent and unique security and video surveillance control.
A processor’s standard operating procedures shall:
Be available to each agent in a form the agent understands;
Be available on-site for inspection by the Administration; and
Accurately reflect the procedures used at the premises.

para.text: A dispensary licensed under COMAR 14.17.06.08 shall:
Be located in Maryland;
Conform to all local zoning and planning requirements;
Conspicuously display valid proof of dispensary licensure; and
Provide accommodations for qualifying patients and registered caregivers in accordance with COMAR 14.17.04.05.
A dispensary may not make modifications or renovations to the standard dispensary premises licensed under COMAR 14.17.06.08 without the prior approval of Maryland Cannabis Administration.
The Administration may require a dispensary licensed under this regulation to conspicuously display:
Minimum purchase age and identification requirements; and
Other information related to health and safety provided by the Administration.
Any display required by the Administration under §C of this regulation shall be in a manner and size determined by the Administration.
A dispensary shall restrict access to the licensed premises to only:
Qualifying patients;
Registered caregivers; and
Individuals 21 years old or older.
Notwithstanding §E of this regulation, a dispensary may allow children younger than 8 years old to enter the licensed premises if they are accompanied for the duration of their visit by a:
Qualifying patient; or
Registered caregiver.
A standard dispensary premises shall:
Be constructed to prevent unauthorized entry; and
Contain a secure room to store the cannabis inventory, which:
Shall be constructed of concrete or similar building material that prevents unauthorized entry;
May not be placed adjacent to an exterior wall of the premises; and
Shall have only one entrance door that:
Prevents unauthorized entry;
Is equipped with a cipher or chip-activated keyed lock or equivalent; and
Is not visible from public areas of the premises.
A dispensary shall store its cannabis inventory in the secure room other than while the licensed dispensary is open for business and 2 hours before and 2 hours after.
A dispensary may request an exemption to §H of this regulation for the purposes of conducting inventory review. Requests for an exemption shall be:
Made in a manner prescribed by the Administration; and
Explicitly authorized by the Administration prior to the exemption.
The licensed dispensary shall design and install lighting fixtures to ensure proper surveillance.
Security Alarm Systems.
A dispensary shall maintain a security alarm system that covers all:
Perimeter entry points and portals at all premises;
Rooms that hold cannabis;
Locations where records are stored on-site; and
Locations where records are stored off-site.
A security system shall:
Be continuously monitored;
Be capable of detecting smoke and fire;
Be capable of detecting power loss;
Include panic alarm devices mounted at convenient, readily accessible locations throughout the licensed premises;
Remain operational until a licensed premises no longer has any cannabis, seeds, or cuttings on the premises; and
Be equipped with auxiliary power sufficient to maintain operation for at least 48 hours.
Video Surveillance Requirements.
A dispensary shall maintain a motion-activated video surveillance recording system at all premises that:
Records all activity in images of high quality and high resolution capable of clearly revealing facial detail;
Operates 24-hours a day, 365 days a year without interruption; and
Provides a date and time stamp for every recorded frame.
A dispensary shall post appropriate notices advising visitors of the video surveillance.
A surveillance camera shall be located and operated to capture each exit from the premises.
A surveillance camera shall capture activity at each:
Entrance to an area where cannabis is packaged, stored, or dispensed; and
Area where cannabis is packaged, stored, or dispensed.
Recordings of security video surveillance shall be:
Access-limited;
Secured by a security alarm system that is independent of the main premises security alarm system;
In a format that can be easily accessed for investigational purposes; and
Retained for a minimum of 90 calendar days.
Any recording of security video surveillance shall be made available to the Administration or law enforcement agency for just cause as requested within 48 hours.
Violation.
Failure to provide the Administration with any recording of video surveillance within 48 hours of a request from the Administration is a violation of COMAR 14.17.14.04.
Each day of recording within the timeframe stated in §L(5)(d) that a dispensary fails to provide to the Administration constitutes a separate violation.
Standard Dispensary Premises Organization.
A dispensary premises shall be divided into a public zone and an operations zone.
Public Zone.
The public zone shall have:
A waiting area open to the general public; and
A service area in which an adult-use consumer, qualifying patient, or caregiver may consult with a dispensary agent and receive cannabis.
The dispensary shall restrict entry into the service area to verified adult-use consumers, qualifying patients, and registered caregivers.
Operations Zone.
All operations other than consulting with adult-use consumers, qualifying patients, and registered caregivers and dispensing cannabis shall be carried out in the operations zone.
The operations zone shall be appropriately divided into separate areas for:
Cannabis storage;
Cannabis preparation and packaging;
Use by dispensary agents for breaks; and
Changing clothing and dispensary agent lockers.
Tamper-evident logbooks or electronic identification logs shall document the movement of persons to and from the operations zone.
Appropriate signage shall clearly delineate the separate zones.
Doors and other access points between zones shall be secured.
Security alarms systems and video surveillance, as described in §§K and L of this regulation, shall be used to monitor the separation between zones.
All cannabis, other than that being displayed, packaged, or dispensed during hours of operation, shall be kept in a secure room that complies with §G(2) of this regulation.
No individual other than a dispensary agent may handle the inventory in a display case or elsewhere in the dispensary until dispensed.
Visitors.
When a visitor is admitted to the operations zone of the premises, a registered dispensary agent shall:
Log the visitor in and out;
Retain with the log a photocopy of the visitor’s government-issued identification;
Continuously, physically supervise the visitor while they are on the premises; and
Ensure that the visitor does not touch any cannabis.
The dispensary shall maintain a log of all visitors to the operations zone for 2 years.
An Administration investigator is not subject to the visitor requirements in this section.

A firefighter, a rescue squad member, law enforcement officer, or emergency medical services personnel who is actively responding to a call for emergency services is not subject to §N(1)(a)—(c) of this regulation.
Drive-through or Walk-up Dispensing Services.
A dispensary providing drive-through or walk-up dispensing services shall:

Dispense through a window or other opening in the exterior wall of a dispensary that enables a registered dispensary agent to serve an adult-use consumer, a qualifying patient, or a registered caregiver without a:
Registered dispensary agent leaving the dispensary service area; or
If applicable, qualifying patient, registered caregiver, or adult-use consumer leaving their vehicle;
Pass a pre-operation inspection before use;
Meet all other requirements for a dispensary service area, including:
The window or opening in the exterior wall shall be constructed to prevent unauthorized entry;
Security lighting, alarms systems, and video surveillance, as described in this regulation;
Displaying the licensed dispensary’s license and hours of operation at the entrance to the drive-through dispensing zone; and
Secured doors and other access points between public and operations zones; and
Adhere to its standard operating procedures and requirements for dispensing cannabis established in Regulation .04 of this chapter.
Fulfill pre-orders in compliance with Regulation .08 of this chapter through the drive-through or walk-up dispensing method.
A dispensary that provides both drive-through and walk-up dispensing services shall designate separate windows or openings for each type of service.
To provide curbside pick-up, a standard dispensary shall:
Designate curbside pick-up parking spots within 100 feet from the dispensary’s entrance and ensure that dispensary agents only deliver cannabis or cannabis products to the designated parking spots;
Capture the on-site delivery on its motion-activated video surveillance recording system as required in §L of this regulation;
Fulfill pre-orders in compliance with Regulation .08 of this chapter; and
Adhere to:
Its standard operating procedures; and
Dispensing requirements in Regulation .04 of this chapter.
Cannabis Display. A dispensary may store cannabis in a clear, tamper-evident canister for display purposes, provided the following conditions are met:
During hours of operation, the canister is:
Stored out of reach behind the service area counter when not being actively examined by a qualifying patient, registered caregiver, or adult-use consumer; or
Otherwise stored securely to prevent unauthorized removal of the canister;
Outside of hours of operation, the canister is stored in the dispensary’s secure room;
Once the display is no longer in use, the dispensary green wastes the cannabis in accordance with its standard operating procedures;
Product stored inside the display jars is not easily accessible or removable by qualifying patients, registered caregivers, or adult-use consumers; and
Cannabis used for display is recorded in accordance with seed-to-sale tracking system procedures.
A micro dispensary may store its inventory in up to three storage facilities:
That it controls and operates, in accordance with §D of this regulation;
Controlled and operated by a licensed grower, processor, or incubator space, in accordance with §D of this regulation; or
Controlled and operated by a licensed standard dispensary, in accordance with Regulation .02 of this chapter.
A micro dispensary storing inventory in a storage facility controlled and operated by another licensee shall:
Ensure each licensee’s cannabis inventory is physically separated and only accessible to the licensee’s agents and owners;

Have equal, independent, and unique control over access to its cannabis inventory, including the ability to add or remove its badged agents to access any area or container in which their cannabis or cannabis products are stored; and
Have equal independent and unique access to its video surveillance recordings, which shall be recorded and saved as required by §K of this regulation or COMAR 14.17.12.02L, whichever applies.

A micro dispensary shall ensure any cannabis or cannabis product has been transferred within the seed-to-sale tracking system to the control of the micro dispensary as prescribed in COMAR 14.17.09.03 prior to:
Any delivery of the cannabis or cannabis product;
Any transportation of the cannabis or cannabis product to a storage facility; and
Any receipt of cannabis or cannabis product to a storage facility.
Except as specified in §A(3) of this regulation, a micro dispensary storage facility shall adhere to the following requirements:
The storage facility shall:
Be located in Maryland;
Maintain relevant and current building codes and standards;
Be constructed to prevent unauthorized entry;
In areas where cannabis is stored, provide adequate lighting, ventilation, temperature, humidity, space, and equipment to maintain product quality;
Be maintained in a clean and orderly condition; and
Be free from infestation by insects, rodents, birds, and pests of any kind.
The micro dispensary shall:
Prohibit access to the storage facility to anyone other than:
The micro dispensary owner;
The micro dispensary agents; and
If applicable, the owner and agents of the licensee that controls or operates the storage facility;
Ensure the storage facility is securely locked and protected from entry, except for the actual time required to remove, replace, or repackage cannabis;
Design and install lighting fixtures to ensure proper surveillance;
Maintain a security alarm system that covers all:
Perimeter entry points and portals at all premises;
Rooms that hold cannabis;
Locations where records are stored on-site; and
Locations where records are stored off-site;
Ensure that the security system in §C(2)(d) of this regulation shall:
Be continuously monitored;
Be capable of detecting smoke and fire;
Be capable of detecting power loss;
Include panic alarm devices mounted at convenient, readily accessible locations throughout the licensed premises;
Remain operational until a licensed premises no longer has any cannabis, seeds, or cuttings on the premises; and
Be equipped with auxiliary power sufficient to maintain operation for at least 48 hours;
Maintain a motion-activated video surveillance recording system that:
Records all activity in images of high quality and high resolution capable of clearly revealing facial detail;
Operates 24-hours a day, 365 days a year without interruption;
Provides a date and time stamp for every recorded frame;
Shall capture activity at each area and entrance to an area where cannabis is packaged, stored, or prepared for transport; and
Keep all locks and security equipment, including video surveillance and alarm systems, in good working order.
Delivery Operations.

A micro dispensary shall verify that the individual placing the order is a consumer who is at least 21 years old, a qualifying patient, or a registered caregiver prior to order placement, using an ordering system that complies with Regulation .08 of this chapter.
A micro dispensary shall only travel:
Between licensees, registrants, or from one delivery address to another delivery address; and
In Maryland.
A micro dispensary shall only make deliveries to residences as defined in COMAR 14.17.01 and medical facilities in the authorized service area.
Agents conducting deliveries shall have on their person:
Their current, active driver’s license; and
Administration-issued proof of the micro dispensary’s licensure and their own registration.
While in transit, delivery agents may not wear any clothing or symbols that may indicate ownership or possession of cannabis.
Each secure cannabis transport vehicle shall be operated by at least one registered agent.
Only registered agents may occupy the delivery vehicle while the vehicle contains cannabis or cannabis products.
All cannabis shall be transported in one or more locked and secure storage containers which may not be accessible while in transit.
A micro dispensary agent shall transport cannabis or cannabis products in an enclosed vehicle that:
Has and displays a current vehicle registration;
Is insured as required by law;
Is equipped with:
A global positioning system (GPS) that enables the micro dispensary to monitor the vehicle’s location for the entirety of its delivery routes;
Locked and secure storage containers anchored to the inside of the vehicle; and
A motion-activated video-surveillance system that captures activity around all storage containers that contain cannabis or cannabis products;
Conceals storage containers so they are not visible or identifiable from outside of the vehicle; and
Does not display any sign or illustration related to cannabis, cannabis products, or a cannabis licensee.
A micro dispensary shall:

Timely and accurately track its stock of cannabis in the seed-to-sale tracking system from the time it is acquired by the micro dispensary to the time it is delivered and dispensed to a qualifying patient, registered caregiver, or adult-use consumer;
Ensure that all cannabis other than that being packaged or dispensed during hours of operation is kept in the storage facility;
Follow its standard operating procedure for examining at specified regular interval the GPS records of all delivery vehicles, including stops made along the delivery route.

When purchasing cannabis from another licensee, coordinate with the licensee to create an electronic manifest to record the chain of custody for each cannabis shipment in accordance with COMAR 14.17.09.03E and F.
A micro dispensary licensed to dispense cannabis under COMAR 14.17.07.07 may not:
Transport cannabis or cannabis products not associated with an active invoice, order, or product manifest;
Transport cannabis or cannabis products in any vehicle noncompliant with §D(10) of this regulation;
Dispense cannabis or cannabis products without a prior order submitted to the licensed business;
Dispense cannabis or cannabis products from the storage facility premises; or
Carry cannabis or cannabis products in the delivery vehicle with a value in excess of $5,000 at any time.

For the purposes §G(5) of this regulation, the value of cannabis goods shall be determined using the current retail price of all cannabis or cannabis products carried by or within the delivery vehicle.
A micro dispensary owner shall be a registered agent if they work or volunteer in any operational areas of the premises, including delivery vehicles.
Global Positioning System (GPS) and Video Surveillance Record Requirements.
GPS and video surveillance recordings required in this regulation shall be:
Access-limited;
In a format that can be easily accessed for investigational purposes;
Retained for a minimum of 90 calendar days; and
Made available to the Administration or law enforcement agency for just cause as requested within 48 hours.
Violation.
Failure to provide the Administration with any GPS or video surveillance record within 48 hours of a request from the Administration is a violation of COMAR 14.17.14.04.
Each day of recording within the timeframe stated in §J(1)(c) of this regulation that a micro dispensary fails to provide to the Administration constitutes a separate violation.
Visitors.
When a visitor is admitted to the operations zone of the premises, a registered dispensary agent shall:
Log the visitor in and out;
Retain with the log a photocopy of the visitor’s government-issued identification;
Continuously, physically supervise the visitor while they are on the premises; and
Ensure that the visitor does not touch any cannabis.
The dispensary shall maintain a log of all visitors to the operations zone for 2 years.
An Administration investigator is not subject to the visitor requirements in this section.
A firefighter, a rescue squad member, or emergency services personnel who is actively responding to a call for emergency services is not subject to §K(1)(a)—(c) of this regulation.
A dispensary shall establish standard operating procedures in accordance with this subtitle for all aspects of:
Inventory control, including:

Tracking the dispensary’s stock of cannabis in the seed-to-sale tracking system from the time it is received by the dispensary to the time it is delivered or dispensed to another licensee, a registrant, a qualifying patient, a registered caregiver, or an adult-use consumer; and
Monthly physical inventory;
Receiving cannabis or products containing cannabis;
Receipt, storage, and distribution of edible cannabis products;
Safe and sanitary storage of cannabis;
Equipment cleaning, maintenance, calibration;
Handling, packaging, and labeling cannabis and cannabis products;
Green waste procedures;
Security and visitor procedures, including theft and diversion prevention;
Storage, handling, and disposing of recalled materials;
Dispensing cannabis and cannabis products to qualifying patients, registered caregivers, and adult-use consumers; and
If applicable:
Delivering cannabis to a qualifying patient, a registered caregiver, or an adult-use consumer;
Repackaging cannabis into usable cannabis products;
Providing reduced cost or discount cannabis or cannabis products to its agents;
Drive-through or walk-up window dispensing;
Curbside dispensing; and
Examining at a specified regular interval GPS records of all delivery vehicles, including stops made along the delivery route.
A dispensary’s standard operating procedures shall:
Be available to each agent in a form the agent understands;
Be available on-site for inspection by the Administration; and
Accurately reflect the procedures used at the premises.

para.text: General Requirements.

Before engaging in the business of possessing, processing, packaging, labeling, transferring, transporting, selling, or distributing edible cannabis products to a dispensary, a licensed processor shall obtain a permit from the Administration in accordance with COMAR 14.17.11.05B.

Prior to offering the products for distribution or sale to a licensed dispensary, a permittee shall obtain approval from the Administration for all edible cannabis products by submitting a request in in a manner prescribed by the Administration.
A permittee seeking approval to offer an edible cannabis product shall submit:
A photograph, digital image, or digital rendering of the product, labeling, and packaging;
The varying levels of potency and dosing of the edible cannabis product;
The recipe, including the production process, for manufacturing the edible cannabis product; and
Any scientific studies or laboratory testing results supporting the stability and approximate expiration date of the edible cannabis product.
The Administration shall review and approve each edible cannabis product before the product may be commercially manufactured or sold by a permittee, to ensure the:
Product complies with the requirements of this chapter; and
Safety of minors.
Dosage Requirements.
Unless expressly authorized by the Administration, an edible cannabis product may not contain more than:
10 milligrams of THC per serving; and
100 milligrams of THC per package.

Each single serving contained in a package of a multiple-serving edible cannabis product shall be physically separated in a way that enables an individual to determine how much of the edible cannabis product constitutes a single serving.
An intact product form containing multiple servings does not meet the requirements of §B(2) of this regulation if it has demarcations or delineations on the product to indicate individual servings.
The Administration may approve a request to manufacture a high potency edible cannabis product containing more than 10 milligrams of THC per serving or 100 milligrams of THC per package if:
A permittee submits a request in a form prescribed by the Administration; and
The request complies with §G of this regulation.
An edible cannabis product consisting of multiple servings shall be homogenized so that each serving contains the same concentration of THC.
Appearance of Edible Cannabis Products.
A permittee shall only manufacture or distribute solid edible cannabis product in geometric shapes.
A permittee may not manufacture an edible cannabis product that due to its shape, design, or flavor is likely to appeal to minors.
The manufacture or sale of edibles in the following shapes is prohibited:
Human, animal, toy, or fruit;
A shape that bears the likeness or contains characteristics of a realistic or fictional human, animal, or fruit, including artistic, caricature, or cartoon renderings; and
A commercially available food or beverage product that targets or is primarily marketed to minors.
Liquid Edible Product Requirements.
A liquid edible product container may not contain more than a single serving per container.
A single serving of a liquid edible product may not exceed 10 milligrams of THC per container.
A liquid edible product shall otherwise comply with this regulation.
Prohibited Products.
Edible cannabis products may not contain:
Meat;
Seafood;
Unpasteurized eggs; or
Unpasteurized dairy of any type.
The following types of products may not be sold:
Alcoholic beverages, as defined in Alcoholic Beverage and Cannabis Article, §1-101, Annotated Code of Maryland; and

Any product containing any non-cannabis additive that would increase potency or toxicity, or that would create an unsafe combination with other psychoactive substances, including nicotine and caffeine.
The prohibition in §E(2)(b) of this regulation does not apply to products containing naturally occurring caffeine, such as coffee, tea, or chocolate.

Compliance with State and Federal Food Safety Requirements. In addition to the other requirements set forth in this chapter, a permittee shall comply with all applicable food safety regulations, including:
21 CFR, as amended;
21 U.S.C. §343, as amended;
21 U.S.C. §§451—471, as amended; and
21 U.S.C. §§601—695, as amended.
High Potency Edible Cannabis Products.
Edible products produced with approval by the Administration under §B(4) of this regulation shall comply with Current Good Manufacturing Practices under 21 CFR Part 111 or 21 CFR Part 210.
A permittee shall submit to the Administration a third-party certification that demonstrates compliance with §G(1) of this regulation.
Demonstration of compliance required under §G(2) of this regulation shall include:
Proof of the third party’s accreditation to certify for Current Good Manufacturing Practices that complies with 21 CFR Part 111 or 21 CFR Part 210;

An attestation that the third party performed a facility audit of the licensed processor’s facility using an audit checklist within the scope of accreditation that complies with 21 CFR Part 111 or 21 CFR Part 210;
The audit checklist for cGMP 21 CFR Part 111 or 21 CFR Part 210, facility score, the audit checklist score scale, and a corrective plan to remediate any deficiencies identified during the audit; and
Documentation that the dosage form is recognized by the United States Pharmacopeia, the National Formulary, or the Food and Drug Administration.
Edible products produced with approval by the Administration under §B(4) of this regulation may not contain more than:
40 milligrams of THC per serving; and
400 milligrams of THC per package.
A dispensary shall only sell or dispense high potency edible products to qualified patients and registered caregivers.
A processor shall label high potency edible products in accordance with COMAR 14.17.18.04.
The Administration shall maintain permittee trade secrets in conformity with COMAR 14.17.11.05.

para.text: Diversion.
A licensee, registrant, or agent may not:
Divert cannabis or cannabis products for any unauthorized sale or distribution;
Fail to maintain records in accordance with this subtitle or the licensee’s standard operating procedures;
Fail to accurately track, tag, or otherwise record inventory in the seed-to-sale tracking system; or
Sell or dispense cannabis or cannabis products in a manner that:
Exceeds the personal use amount of cannabis or cannabis products;
Is reasonably expected to be in violation of the personal use amount; or
Could otherwise reasonably give rise to any other violation of law.
A licensee, registrant, or agent may not conduct a series of transactions that would be in violation of §A(1)(d) of this regulation.
Health Endangerment.
A licensee, registrant, or agent may not:
Apply any contaminant to cannabis or cannabis products;
Handle cannabis or cannabis products in a manner that would endanger health and safety;
Store cannabis or cannabis products in a manner that would endanger health and safety; or
Otherwise risk the health and safety of a patient or consumer.

A licensee, registrant, or agent may not sell, dispense, or otherwise distribute cannabis or cannabis products to an individual younger than 21 years old unless the individual is a qualifying patient 18 years old or older.
A licensee or registrant may not substantially deviate or demonstrate a pattern of deviation from:
Its standard operating procedures;
Its application; or
State, federal, or local:
Law;
Regulations; or
Ordinances.
The Administration may fine, suspend, restrict, revoke, or otherwise sanction any cannabis licensee, registrant, or agent for:
Any violation of Alcoholic Beverages and Cannabis Article, Title 36, Annotated Code of Maryland;
Any violation of this subtitle;
Restricting, limiting, or otherwise impeding an inspection or investigation by the Administration;
A material misstatement, omission, misrepresentation, or untruth by the licensee, registrant, registered agent, or other employee; or
Any other violation of State, federal, or local:
Law;
Regulations; or
Ordinances.

para.text: To volunteer or work for a licensee or registrant, an individual shall be registered with the Administration as an agent.
Agent registration shall be conducted pursuant to Alcoholic Beverages and Cannabis Article, §36-501, Annotated Code of Maryland.
A licensee shall apply to register an agent by submitting to the Administration:
The name, address, date of birth, and Social Security Number or Individual Tax Identification Number of an agent;

Documentation of the submission of fingerprints of the agent of to the Criminal Justice Information System’s Central Repository in accordance with Alcoholic Beverages and Cannabis Article, §36-505, Annotated Code of Maryland;
The request for the criminal history record information of the agent to be forwarded to the Administration.
As a condition of registration, an agent shall report to the Administration a conviction of or plea of nolo contendere to a crime involving moral turpitude within 14 days of the conviction or plea.
The Administration may:

Disqualify as an agent an individual who has been convicted of or pleaded nolo contendere to a crime involving moral turpitude, whether or not any appeal or other proceeding is pending to have the conviction or plea set aside;

Revoke the registration of an agent who is convicted of or pleads nolo contendere to a crime involving moral turpitude, whether or not any appeal or other proceeding is pending to have the conviction or plea set aside; and
Disqualify as an agent an individual who previously had a registration revoked under Alcoholic Beverages and Cannabis Article, §36-202(a)(7), Annotated Code of Maryland, or for good cause.
The Administration may not deny a cannabis agent registration based on any cannabis-related offense occurring before July 1, 2023.
The licensee shall train all registered agents on:
Federal and State cannabis laws and regulation and other laws and regulations pertinent to the agent’s responsibilities;
Standard operating procedures;
The State alcohol and drug free workplace policy, as identified in COMAR 21.11.08.03;
Detection and prevention of diversion of cannabis;
Security procedures; and
Safety procedures including responding to:
A medical emergency;
A fire;
A chemical spill; and
A threatening event such as:
An armed robbery;
An invasion;
A burglary; and
Any other criminal incident.
The licensee shall retain training materials and attendance records and make the training materials available for inspection by the Administration.
Within 90 days of employment start date and annually thereafter, a registered agent employed by a cannabis licensee shall complete a responsible vendor training program that:
Meets the minimum requirements under Alcoholic Beverages and Cannabis Article, §§36-1001—36-1003, Annotated Code of Maryland; and
Is registered with the Administration in accordance with §E(3) of this regulation.
A responsible vendor training program required under §E of this regulation shall be in addition to the training requirements under §A of this regulation.
Responsible Vendor Training Program.
To offer a responsible medical or adult-use cannabis vendor, server, and seller training program, a person shall submit an application to the Administration.

To be considered for approval, the proposed training program application shall meet the minimum educational standards established in Alcoholic Beverages and Cannabis Article, §36-1001(c), Annotated Code of Maryland.
Applications approved by the Administration shall be registered with the Administration for a period of 3 years from the date of approval.
The Administration shall assess a fee for the application, registration, and renewal of a responsible vendor training program under this regulation as specified in COMAR 14.17.21.
A person offering a responsible vendor training program under this paragraph may not have ownership or control of any cannabis license.
A person offering a responsible vendor training program shall:
Maintain records for at least 4 years; and A
Make these records available to the Administration upon request.

para.text: Application fees:
For a standard license, on-site consumption license, or incubator space– $5,000; and
For a micro license — $1,000.
Licensing fees:
For a standard license, on-site consumption license, or incubator space issued under COMAR 14.17.06, the licensing fees for initial licensure or renewal shall be:
Grower license — $50,000;
Processor license — $25,000;
Dispensary license — $25,000;
Incubator space license — $10,000; and
On-site consumption license — $10,000.
For a micro license issued under COMAR 14.17.07:
Grower license — $10,000;
Processor license — $10,000; and
Dispensary license — $10,000.
Licensing fees required under this section shall be valid for a 5-year period.

A licensed social equity licensee shall have the licensing fees in §B(1) and (2) of this regulation reduced by 50 percent by the Administration for each license type held by the social equity licensees.
Registration fees:
Cannabis agent fees:
Registration fee to be paid every 2 years —$25; and
Replacement identification card fee — $50.
Independent testing laboratory fees:
Laboratory registration fee to be paid every 2 years — $500;
Employee registration fee to be paid every 2 years —$25; and
Replacement employee identification card fee — $50.
Registrant:
Business registration fee to be paid every 2 years — $100;
Agent registration fee to be paid every 2 years —$25; and
Replacement agent identification card fee — $50.
Research and development fees:
Academic research institution fees:
Registration fee — $500;
Renew fee for each subsequent or modified research project — $100;
Academic research representative registration fee —$25; and
Academic research representative card replacement fee — $50.
Research and development entity fees:
Registration fee — $1,000;
Renewal fee for each subsequent or modified research project — $500;
Research and development representative registration fee — $25; and
Research and development representative card replacement fee — $50.
Responsible vendor training program fees:
Registration fee — $500; and
Renewal fee — $250.
Qualifying patient and caregiver fees:
Identification card base fee — $25; and
Replacement identification card fee — $50.
Micro license conversion fees:
A micro licensee who wishes to convert the micro license into a standard license shall pay a pro-rated amount based on:
The number of whole months remaining on the initial micro license; and
The fee amount difference between a standard and micro license of the license type.
The conversion fee in this section shall be calculated by the Administration using:
For growers:
$50,000 for a full 5-year license term; or
A pro-rated amount for each remaining whole month the initial micro license is valid.
For processors:
$25,000 for a full 5-year license term; or
A pro-rated amount for each remaining whole month the initial micro license is valid.
For dispensaries:
$25,000 for a full 5-year license term; or
A pro-rated amount for each remaining whole month the initial micro license is valid.
The conversion fee calculated under this section shall be paid in full within 18 months of the date the conversion is in effect.
Edible cannabis product permit application fee —$1,000.
Miscellaneous fees:
Transfer of 5 percent or more ownership interest in or control of a grower, processor, dispensary, on-site consumption, or incubator space license with entity transferee — $1,000;
Transfer of 5 percent or more ownership interest in a grower, processor, dispensary, on-site consumption, or incubator space license with individual transferee — $500 per person;
Change in the location of a grower, processor, or dispensary premises – $500;
License reinstatement fee — $2,000;
Receivership application fee — $1,000; and
Management agreement fee — $1,000.
At its discretion, the Administration may waive or reduce the fees established in this regulation.
